title: Andrew

jokes.text: Mike trying to spin the song as 'jesty' and the reporter repeating 'Jesty?' back at him with obvious skepticism
jokes.setup: Mike is doing his best to spin Selina's embarrassing Europe trip, calling the song 'jesty'
jokes.punchline: Leon's flat echo of 'Jesty?' exposes the absurdity of the made-up, desperate spin word
jokes.explanation: Mike's invention of 'jesty' as a spin word is peak Veep — the press flack grasping for vocabulary that doesn't exist. Leon's deadpan repeat is the ideal reaction beat. Highly character-specific to Mike's hapless comms role.

jokes.text: Mike responding to Leon's rapid-fire gotcha questions about the spy coverup by saying 'Is your blood sugar off? Have a pastry or some herring, okay?'
jokes.setup: Leon has just exposed a genuine contradiction in Selina's hostage rescue story, pressing Mike hard
jokes.punchline: Mike deflects not with a real answer but by suggesting Leon eat the Finnish breakfast — absurd deflection as crisis comms
jokes.explanation: Deeply character-specific to Mike — rather than address an unanswerable question, he weaponizes the free breakfast. The specificity of 'herring' grounds the absurdity. Reveals Mike's incompetent-but-endearing spin style in one line.

jokes.text: Leon's systematic dismantling: 'Either she knew the student was a spy and lied when she denied it, or she didn't know, but lied about knowing the details of the military rescue.' Mike's response: 'This isn't Meyer the Liar again.' Leon: 'Well, it is now.'
jokes.setup: Mike has tried to brush off the spy revelation; Leon calmly lays out that both possible explanations involve lying
jokes.punchline: 'This isn't Meyer the Liar again.' / 'Well, it is now.' — the callback nickname lands like a verdict
jokes.explanation: The rhyme 'Meyer the Liar' does real comedic work — it's both a real political attack and an absurd schoolyard taunt. Leon's four-word confirmation is structurally perfect. Sets up a running gag for the episode.

jokes.text: Selina's negotiation small talk: 'All you ever want for these kids is to be happy and healthy. And stay out of Middle East politics. So two out of three ain't bad.'
jokes.setup: Selina and Mary King are doing polite small talk about their children
jokes.punchline: The pivot to 'stay out of Middle East politics' as a parenting goal — implying Catherine is already entangled — lands as a dark admission dressed up as folksy wisdom
jokes.explanation: The Meatloaf reference ('two out of three ain't bad') is deployed with just enough self-awareness. The joke works because it's a genuine reveal — Catherine really does have a boyfriend in the Middle East — disguised as a cliché parenting sentiment.

jokes.text: After Catherine's Middle East comment, Selina says 'I'm amazed you can laugh about that.' Selina replies flatly: 'Yes, that's what I do. I'm laughing about it.'
jokes.setup: Someone expresses genuine surprise that Selina can laugh off a geopolitically complicated situation
jokes.punchline: Selina's response is barely even a response — 'Yes, that's what I do. I'm laughing about it.' — the flatness suggests she is absolutely not okay
jokes.explanation: A small but real joke — Selina's automatic coping mechanism stated so plainly it becomes existentially funny. Functional rather than brilliant.

jokes.text: Dan describing waiting for the Mary King meeting outcome: 'It's like waiting for a biopsy result.'
jokes.setup: The staff are anxiously waiting to see how the debt negotiations go
jokes.punchline: The biopsy simile — darkly accurate about the existential stakes of political deal-making
jokes.explanation: Efficient, dark, and precise. The biopsy analogy speaks to the life-or-death political stakes without over-explaining. Good economy.

jokes.text: Mary King pretended to faint to win negotiations against Brian Earl
jokes.setup: Amy is briefing Dan on Mary King's negotiating tactics
jokes.punchline: The revelation that faking a medical emergency is a legitimate and successful political tactic
jokes.explanation: Gets a rewatch bonus because the fainting gambit then actually happens in this episode — so this line functions as a setup for a later payoff. On first watch it's a good character detail; on rewatch it's a planted joke.

jokes.text: Dan's reaction to learning Amy's date is Ed Webster: 'You swore you were only gonna date outside DC.' / Amy: 'He's not in DC. He works in Boston.' / Dan: 'Well, my mom doesn't live in Rome, but she's still a fucking Catholic.'
jokes.setup: Amy tries to defend her choice to date an insider by pointing out Ed technically works in Boston
jokes.punchline: Dan's mother/Catholic analogy — geographically irrelevant affiliation is still affiliation
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The Catholic/Rome analogy is perfectly constructed — the logic is airtight and unexpected, the profanity lands naturally, and it's entirely Dan: weaponizing a quick-draw analogy to make someone feel stupid. Highly quotable. The structure is precise: Amy's technical defense gets immediately dismantled by a left-field but logically irrefutable comparison.

jokes.text: Dan: 'Are you getting worked up, Danny?' / Dan: 'Shut the fuck up, Gary.' / Amy: 'I'm so happy you're happy for me, Dan.' / Dan: 'Oh, what do you want, a fucking cake?'
jokes.setup: Gary needles Dan about being worked up over Amy's date; Amy twists the knife
jokes.punchline: 'What do you want, a fucking cake?' — Dan's furious non-answer confirms exactly what Gary and Amy suspected
jokes.explanation: Dan's wounded pride masked as aggression is very him. 'What do you want, a fucking cake?' is a funny non-sequitur that inadvertently rhymes with the party context. Good rapid-fire ensemble beat.

jokes.text: Selina calls Mary's bluff: 'You stalling for time?' then offers a Kleenex, and adds: 'I think it's over. Sneezes always come in threes. Have you ever noticed that?' Mary: 'Really? Is that so?' — and then Mary sneezes a fourth time.
jokes.setup: Selina confidently states sneezes always come in threes, clearly trying to use folk wisdom to wrap up Mary's distraction
jokes.punchline: Immediate fourth sneeze demolishes her authority
jokes.explanation: Clean structural joke — Selina sets herself up for a fall by invoking folk wisdom with misplaced confidence, and reality immediately punishes her. Selina then gets to say 'So maybe I need to tell you about how counting works' — a good recovery line.

jokes.text: Selina notices Mary may be about to faint: 'You're gonna faint?' Mary: 'I'm not-- oh, very funny.'
jokes.setup: Earlier Amy mentioned Mary faked fainting to win negotiations
jokes.punchline: Selina calls the bluff in real-time — 'You gonna faint?' — referencing the known tactic
jokes.explanation: Pays off the earlier setup about Mary's fainting tactic. Selina knowing and naming it is both funny and characterizes Selina as having done her homework. Mary's 'very funny' confirms the joke landed in-universe too.

jokes.text: Gary's cats cause Mary's allergic reaction — 'Oh, Gary has cats.' — and then 'Gary, deflower the room.'
jokes.setup: Mary is having an allergic reaction; everyone is guessing the cause — mohair, nuts
jokes.punchline: It's Gary's cats (a wonderfully specific detail). Then Selina gives the command 'Gary, deflower the room' about the flowers Gary brought for Catherine's birthday
jokes.explanation: The escalating guessing game is fun, but the best beat is the command 'deflower the room' — which is both a real instruction and an accidentally suggestive phrase. Gary being the allergic culprit is good character comedy: even his thoughtful gesture (birthday flowers) causes a crisis.

jokes.text: 'Gary, deflower the room.' / 'There's a sentence I bet you thought you'd never hear.'
jokes.setup: Selina barks 'Gary, deflower the room' as a literal instruction about flowers
jokes.punchline: Someone notes the unintentional double entendre — 'There's a sentence I bet you thought you'd never hear'
jokes.explanation: The meta-comment on the funny sentence is a second beat on the same joke. Slightly self-congratulatory but charming — and the line itself is genuinely funny enough to merit the lampshade.

jokes.text: Mary insists they must finish the deal tonight; Selina notes she has her daughter's 21st birthday. Mary: 'Would you miss your son's Civil Union ceremony?' / 'I did.' / 'Oh. Well, then, you have to come to Catherine's.'
jokes.setup: Mary uses a hypothetical to pressure Selina — would you miss your kid's Civil Union?
jokes.punchline: Mary's flat confession that she actually did miss her son's ceremony — instantly demolishing her own moral leverage, then Selina flipping it back on her
jokes.explanation: Exceptional two-beat structure: Mary invokes a rhetorical hypothetical to guilt Selina, then is immediately caught by her own trap. 'I did' is the devastating confession, and Selina's pivot — 'Well, then, you have to come to Catherine's' — is the perfect logical judo. Tight, surprising, dark, and totally character-consistent for both politicians.

jokes.text: 'Stick a coat and tie on a fucking oxygen tank.'
jokes.setup: Mary says she'll bring a plus one to the party; presumably she means someone to help with her allergic reaction
jokes.punchline: Selina's immediate, deadpan suggestion — dress up the oxygen tank as a guest
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout lines. Perfect economy — seven words, completely absurd, and yet logically follows from 'bring a plus one.' The visual of a dressed-up oxygen tank as a party guest is vivid and ridiculous. Highly quotable outside context. This is the kind of throwaway that Iannucci rooms live for.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Some dumbass is resurrecting Meyer the Liar.' / Mike: 'Ugh, just because my last name rhymes with liar.'
jokes.setup: They're discussing the return of the 'Meyer the Liar' attack
jokes.punchline: Mike makes it about himself — his last name rhymes with liar — missing the point entirely while also being correct
jokes.explanation: Mike McLintock narcissism at its finest — in the middle of a political crisis, he centers his own name. The move is pure Mike: technically correct, totally beside the point, and weirdly self-pitying. Excellent character integration.

jokes.text: Mike: 'How lucky am I that I'm not Selina Mapist or Selina Medophile?'
jokes.setup: Mike is riffing on rhymes with liar — names that could be even more damaging
jokes.punchline: 'Selina Medophile' — the escalation into something genuinely horrifying, delivered with oblivious cheerfulness
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation done right — Mike's cheerful rhyme-spinning leads him to 'Medophile' (pedophile), which he says with the same light tone as 'Mapist.' The gap between the horror of the word and Mike's breezy delivery is where the laugh lives. Iannucci at his most transgressive-but-earned.

jokes.text: Jonah: 'I just saw Mary King. Looked like she's been thrown up by a whale.'
jokes.setup: Jonah arrives and comments on Mary King's post-allergic-reaction appearance
jokes.punchline: 'Thrown up by a whale' — a vivid, specific image for looking terrible
jokes.explanation: A beautifully specific image. Not just 'looked sick' — 'thrown up by a whale.' The direction (the whale rejected her) makes it even better. Economy is strong.

jokes.text: 'If we don't get this done by midnight, we're all gonna turn into ugly sisters and get eaten by wolves or whatever the fuck happens at the end of that story.'
jokes.setup: Ben is warning about the midnight deadline for the debt deal
jokes.punchline: He mangles the Cinderella fairy tale by mixing in Little Red Riding Hood and then admits he doesn't know the ending
jokes.explanation: The escalating mangling of fairy tales — 'ugly sisters' (Cinderella) gets eaten by wolves (Little Red Riding Hood) — capped by the honest admission 'or whatever the fuck happens at the end of that story,' is a perfect Ben moment. He's powerful enough not to care about getting the reference right. The 'ugly sisters' note is also a mild self-own. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Amy receives the news that Ed Webster is here. Jonah: 'I think he's nervous. His voice sounded clammy.'
jokes.setup: Jonah is relaying that Ed arrived and seems nervous
jokes.punchline: 'His voice sounded clammy' — a synesthetic malapropism; 'clammy' is a tactile word applied to sound
jokes.explanation: Jonah's malapropisms are a reliable vein of comedy. 'Clammy voice' is good — it's almost right but completely wrong in a way that reveals Jonah's dim but not-quite-zero intelligence.

jokes.text: Ben to Amy, completely casually: 'It's good that you'll have sex soon.'
jokes.setup: Selina says Amy looks flustered about her date with Ed
jokes.punchline: Ben's approval — framed not as romantic encouragement but as an efficiency observation about Amy needing release
jokes.explanation: Ben's total lack of social filter delivered with absolute sincerity is a highlight. The line is funny because it's framed as logistical approval rather than TMI — this is simply a problem being solved. Very Ben, very Veep. High economy and strong character integration.

jokes.text: Press release version two: 'Mary King steals peace pipe, breaks it in half, shits all over it.'
jokes.setup: They need to prep two versions of the press release — success or failure
jokes.punchline: The failure version is not diplomatic at all — a hilariously petty, vindictive summary of Mary King's actions
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'steals peace pipe' to 'breaks it in half' to 'shits all over it' is good three-beat comedy. The absurdity of calling this a press release is the real joke. Classic Veep: high-stakes politics described in playground terms.

jokes.text: Andrew gave Selina a black Porsche for Valentine's Day that he bought with Catherine's trust fund money. 'He just fluffs ya, and then he fucks ya.'
jokes.setup: Selina asks 'Can I ask why?' about hating Andrew; Gary says 'This'll explain it perfectly' and tells the Porsche story
jokes.punchline: 'He just fluffs ya, and then he fucks ya' — crude but rhythmically perfect summary of Andrew's MO
jokes.explanation: Gets a rewatch bonus because the 'fluff/fuck' formulation returns later when Andrew's PAC betrayal is revealed. As character establishment for Andrew it's efficient and memorable. The crude rhyme makes it sticky.

jokes.text: Gary explaining Selina's Andrew-effect to Amy/Dan: 'It's like a cat on a hot tin dog.'
jokes.setup: Gary is trying to describe what happens to Selina around Andrew — she gets giddy
jokes.punchline: 'It's like a cat on a hot tin dog' — a mangled version of 'cat on a hot tin roof' that's somehow funnier for being wrong
jokes.explanation: Gary's mangled idiom is one of his defining comedy tools. 'Cat on a hot tin dog' is wrong in just the right way — 'tin roof' becomes 'tin dog,' which makes no sense but is somehow funnier than the original. Very Gary: earnest, imprecise, almost illuminating.

jokes.text: Selina: 'I'm gonna be the sexiest woman to ever exude fiscal prudence. And that's a very fucking tough look to pull off.'
jokes.setup: Selina is getting ready for Catherine's party where she'll also see Andrew and close the debt deal
jokes.punchline: The absurd juxtaposition of 'sexiest woman' and 'fiscal prudence' — and the accurate acknowledgment that yes, it IS a tough look
jokes.explanation: Quintessential Selina Meyer — the collision of her vanity and her political ambition in a single sentence. 'Exude fiscal prudence' is an inspired phrase. The self-aware tag ('that's a very tough look to pull off') saves it from being just boasting and makes it genuinely charming. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Ed's party warning: 'Fair warning, I party pretty hard. You should see me dance. I can't be controlled. I do a lot of pelvic thrusts. Just very localized dancing. Very small orbits.'
jokes.setup: Ed is trying to seem exciting and fun before Amy's date
jokes.punchline: The escalating self-description collapses from 'I can't be controlled' to 'very localized dancing. Very small orbits.' — revealing a deeply dorky man
jokes.explanation: The de-escalation structure works perfectly: each additional detail undercuts the previous claim. 'Very small orbits' is a great phrase — precise, scientific, sad. Ed immediately establishes himself as a lovable dork.

jokes.text: Ed: 'I've been wrecking parties since I was 13. I was like the Hunter S. Thompson of bar mitzvahs.'
jokes.setup: Ed continues his self-mythologizing about his legendary party presence
jokes.punchline: 'The Hunter S. Thompson of bar mitzvahs' — an absurd mash-up of gonzo excess and Jewish coming-of-age ceremonies
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best single lines. The specificity of 'bar mitzvahs' rather than just 'parties' is what elevates it — it grounds the absurdity in an oddly specific cultural setting. The image of Hunter S. Thompson at a bar mitzvah is both vivid and hilarious. Great economy: five words that build a whole character backstory.

jokes.text: Selina meeting Ed: 'We met in... in Bumfuck, Idaho, I think it was.' / Ed: 'DC.' / Selina: 'Yes, yes. That's what I like to call DC as a joke.'
jokes.setup: Selina doesn't remember Ed despite him working for her for five years
jokes.punchline: She calls DC 'Bumfuck, Idaho' — accidentally revealing her contempt for the city she works in — then desperately claims it's a joke
jokes.explanation: Reveals multiple things at once: Selina doesn't know her own staff, she's socially panicked when caught, and her attempt to save face ('that's what I like to call DC as a joke') is transparently false and only makes it worse. 'Bumfuck, Idaho' is a great instinct — the obscenity makes the slip worse. Classic Veep character comedy.

jokes.text: Dan to Ed after Selina fails to remember him: 'Hey buddy, don't worry that she didn't remember you, okay? It's only 'cause you're a nonentity.'
jokes.setup: Dan is ostensibly comforting Ed about Selina not recognizing him
jokes.punchline: The 'comfort' is more insulting than the original slight — 'only 'cause you're a nonentity'
jokes.explanation: Pure Dan Egan — offers a fake comfort that's actually a sharper insult than the original injury. The word 'nonentity' is perfectly chosen: clinical, total, dismissive. Dan's sociopathy is on full display.

jokes.text: Selina to Catherine: 'Don't you think that you and your friends would call this whole thing an epic succeed?'
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to seem cool and with-it to her 21-year-old daughter at the party
jokes.punchline: 'An epic succeed' — mangling youth slang (should be 'epic win' or just 'epic'); Selina is deeply uncool while convinced she's cool
jokes.explanation: Selina's failed attempt at millennial speak — 'epic succeed' instead of 'epic win' — is a precise character joke. She's the kind of person who's heard the phrase but hasn't quite got it. Catherine's noncommittal 'something like that, yeah' is the perfect deadpan response.

jokes.text: Andrew asking 'you still running marathons? Right, surprised you still got any nipples left.'
jokes.setup: Andrew is doing his thing of remembering one personal detail about each guest
jokes.punchline: He pairs the marathon compliment with an unprompted medical observation about marathon runner's nipple chafing
jokes.explanation: Andrew's charm offensive is immediately subverted by the nipple joke — he goes just a beat too far, which characterizes him perfectly as someone who thinks his charm licenses anything. The image is vivid and specific.

jokes.text: Andrew to Jenny at the party: 'Remember that one time when you said you were way into Twilight and I thought you meant the time of day? It really is the most dangerous time for traffic, though.'
jokes.setup: Andrew is doing his charming 'remember one little thing about each person' routine
jokes.punchline: He both demonstrates recall of a specific conversation AND reveals he genuinely didn't understand cultural references — then defends his misunderstanding with a sincere observation about traffic
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional. The joke operates on multiple levels: Andrew proves he IS good with people (he remembered the conversation) while also revealing he's not quite in the cultural loop. Then the clincher — 'It really is the most dangerous time for traffic, though' — delivered with complete sincerity, makes it clear he wasn't joking at the time. Perfect character establishment for Andrew as both charming and fundamentally obtuse.

jokes.text: Catherine: 'Mom, you've invited the House Majority Leader to my birthday party?' / Selina: 'Yeah, sweetheart, it's the 100 billion cutoff. You've heard about that?' / 'Of course I've heard about that.' / 'Okay, so we are fixing the biggest problem facing the world right here, right now at your party. So that's kind of cool, don't you think?'
jokes.setup: Catherine is justifiably upset that her 21st birthday party has been turned into a fiscal summit
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'So that's kind of cool, don't you think?' — attempting to frame ruining her daughter's party as something exciting for Catherine
jokes.explanation: Classic Selina obliviousness — reframing her own selfishness as a gift to Catherine. The fact that Catherine already knows about the deal (she's politically informed) makes it weirder and funnier.

jokes.text: Selina to Rahim: 'You drink alcohol?' / 'Yes, ma'am.' / 'Oh, you Iranians are full of surprises.'
jokes.setup: Selina offers champagne to Catherine's Iranian boyfriend Rahim, then is surprised he accepts
jokes.punchline: 'You Iranians are full of surprises' — a clueless ethnic generalization from Selina that reveals both her prejudice and her attempt to seem cosmopolitan
jokes.explanation: Selina's casual diplomatic ignorance is perfectly rendered. The pause before 'You Iranians are full of surprises' suggests she genuinely thinks this is a compliment. Then the immediate cut to them not understanding what he said in Farsi ('Some gibberish. I can't understand him.') deepens the cringe beautifully.

jokes.text: After Rahim says 'Salâmati' (cheers in Farsi), Selina and her aide: 'What did he just say?' / 'I don't know. Some gibberish. I can't understand him.'
jokes.setup: Rahim responds to 'Cheers' in Farsi — a completely normal thing to do
jokes.punchline: Selina's team immediately dismisses it as 'gibberish' — the ugly American abroad moment, in her own country
jokes.explanation: Works as a one-two punch with the previous joke — Selina's superficial multiculturalism immediately deflated by ignorance. 'I can't understand him' about a man speaking perfectly comprehensible Farsi to someone who speaks English is quietly devastating.

jokes.text: Ben arrives with 'number crunchers to help you finish the deal. And one of them is a designated driver because I am going to get fucked up.'
jokes.setup: Ben has crashed the birthday party with support staff to help close the deal
jokes.punchline: The professional justification for the team ('help you finish the deal') is immediately undercut by Ben's personal reason for being there: to get wasted at a 21-year-old's party
jokes.explanation: Ben's nihilistic self-interest is perfectly rendered. The designated driver detail is key — he planned this. He didn't accidentally decide to drink; he prepared to get drunk at a young woman's birthday party. Classic Ben.

jokes.text: Someone flattering Selina about her appearance: 'You look so good, you're like a work of art. You make this stuff look like shit. Compared to you, that Monet is a piece of shit.'
jokes.setup: Gary is complimenting Selina as she arrives at the party/gallery
jokes.punchline: The escalating compliment goes from 'work of art' to dismissing a Monet as 'a piece of shit' compared to her — the hyperbole becomes absurdist
jokes.explanation: Gary's sycophancy escalated to its logical extreme — he's not just complimenting Selina, he's condemning Impressionist masterworks to bolster her ego. The Monet specifically is a good choice: recognizable, aspirationally cultured.

jokes.text: Amy trying to limit Selina's Andrew time: 'The ex? Three minutes.' / Selina: 'What, are you negotiating already? I'll give you four.' / Amy: 'I'll cut her off at three, okay?'
jokes.setup: The team is trying to limit Selina's exposure to Andrew
jokes.punchline: Selina instinctively negotiates her own allotted time with her ex-husband — then Amy ignores the negotiation entirely and sticks to three minutes
jokes.explanation: The meta-comedy of a politician applying negotiation tactics to her personal life is pitch-perfect. Selina can't turn it off. Amy's aside to Gary — 'I'll cut her off at three' — treating Selina's counter-offer as irrelevant is the right button on top.

jokes.text: Andrew about scuba diving: 'Magical to be down there. With the coral and the fish. Feeling up all the mermaids. They should cover up if they don't want it.'
jokes.setup: Andrew is describing the wonders of scuba diving in Grand Cayman
jokes.punchline: The pivot from genuine wonder ('magical to be down there') to 'feeling up all the mermaids. They should cover up if they don't want it.' — casually revealing Andrew's sexual entitlement through fictional creatures
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best Andrew moments. The joke uses fantasy creatures to make a real (and uncomfortable) character point — Andrew's casual, semi-joking sexism is revealed through his treatment of fictional mermaids. 'They should cover up if they don't want it' is both funny and a genuine character indictment. The mermaid framing gives the writers plausible deniability while landing the joke. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Andrew: 'What would your answer be?' / Selina: 'My answer would be Jesus fucking Christ.'
jokes.setup: Andrew has created a romantic moment and Selina has said 'What are you doing? Are you gonna propose?'
jokes.punchline: 'My answer would be Jesus fucking Christ' — not a no, not a yes, but an exasperated oath that conveys the complicated mess of her feelings for him
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's signature lines. 'Jesus fucking Christ' as a marriage proposal response perfectly encapsulates Selina and Andrew's relationship — it's neither yes nor no, neither romantic nor hostile. It's an exasperated capitulation and a comedic grenade at the same time. Absolutely character-specific to Selina.

jokes.text: Gary: 'Well, technically, ma'am, he had the palm of your hand in his hand.' / Selina: 'Oh.' / Gary: 'You need to be really careful, sweetie.'
jokes.setup: Selina claims she 'had Andrew in the palm of her hand'
jokes.punchline: Gary technically corrects her on the hand-holding direction, then accidentally calls her 'sweetie'
jokes.explanation: Gary's inadvertent affection ('sweetie') is the better laugh here, but the setup of gently correcting Selina's self-flattering account is good Gary — he both worships her and can't help telling the truth. The two-part joke (technical correction + accidental 'sweetie') works as an escalating beat.

jokes.text: Gary calls Selina 'sweetie' by accident, then is horrified
jokes.setup: Gary is in the middle of warning Selina to be careful around Andrew
jokes.punchline: 'You need to be really careful, sweetie.' / 'Gary, you just called me sweetie.' / 'Oh, my God! I'm sorry!'
jokes.explanation: The horror of Gary accidentally addressing the VP with a pet name he uses for his partner Dana — revealing the extent to which Selina IS his primary relationship — is a deeply funny and slightly disturbing character moment. Perfect Gary.

jokes.text: Gary: 'Oh my God, I'm sorry.' / Selina: 'That's what I call Dana.' / Gary: 'Did you ever call Dana ma'am?' / Gary: 'I did once and it was awful.'
jokes.setup: Gary has accidentally called Selina 'sweetie' — his girlfriend's name
jokes.punchline: The immediate follow-up — did he ever call Dana 'ma'am'? Yes. Once. And it was awful. — reveals the total collapse of Gary's personal/professional boundary
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best Gary moments. The implication that Gary once called his girlfriend 'ma'am' in a context he describes only as 'awful' is a perfect joke — the audience can imagine the scenario and it's worse than anything they could show. 'It was awful' is perfectly understated. This is deeply character-specific to Gary's complete psychological absorption into Selina's world.

jokes.text: Selina: 'If I were drunk right now, would you kiss me?' / Gary: 'Yeah.' / Selina: 'No, Gary, I'm kidding.' / Gary: 'Right.'
jokes.setup: Selina is in a playful mood after the Gary/'sweetie' exchange
jokes.punchline: Gary answers 'yeah' without hesitation before she clarifies she's kidding — and his 'Right' after is not entirely convincing
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's high-water mark for cringe comedy. Gary's immediate, unguarded 'Yeah' is devastating — it answers too quickly and too honestly. The beat that follows (Selina's clarification, Gary's deflated 'Right') is exquisitely uncomfortable. Every element of their codependent relationship is compressed into four lines. The 'Right' that follows is the best beat in the scene — it doesn't sound like agreement, it sounds like a cover. Masterclass in Veep's cringe-comedy brand.

jokes.text: Ben's party arrival announcement: 'It's time to drop a party bomb up in here!' followed by 'Happy 21st birthday, Catherine!' — immediately followed by proposing to talk about targeted base closings
jokes.setup: Ben makes a grand entrance claiming party energy, then immediately pivots to fiscal policy
jokes.punchline: The gap between 'party bomb' and 'targeted base closings' is the joke — Ben's idea of a party is fiscal negotiations
jokes.explanation: Ben's Washington-brain making him incapable of actual human socializing is a reliable well. The '21st birthday' call being immediately undercut is good juxtaposition.

jokes.text: In the middle of intense negotiations: 'Something sex my dick trick?' / 'Did you say dick?' / 'Sex your dick trick.'
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to close the deal in the middle of a noisy 21st birthday party
jokes.punchline: A negotiating term gets garbled in the noise into something that sounds like sexual gibberish — neither party can make sense of it
jokes.explanation: A quick noise-garble joke. The specific sounds ('sex your dick trick') are funny as a phrase, but the joke is somewhat random. Works in the moment as physical comedy context.

jokes.text: Andrew's double entendre to Selina: 'You look great in an art gallery 'cause you kind of are a work of art. I mean, I know I'd like to nail you up against a wall.' / 'No, that was knowing. I was being knowing.'
jokes.setup: Andrew escalates his compliment to Selina from 'work of art' to 'nail you against a wall'
jokes.punchline: He immediately tries to claim sophistication — 'I was being knowing' — which is a funnier word choice than 'clever'
jokes.explanation: The 'nail against a wall' double entendre is not particularly original, but Andrew's self-defense ('I was being knowing') redeems it — 'knowing' is an odd, slightly pompous word choice that fits Andrew's character perfectly.

jokes.text: Selina interrupting teenage girls' fight to say 'Hey gals, you need to keep it down, okay? There are other people in here trying to have a conversation.' — while she's negotiating federal fiscal policy
jokes.setup: Two teenage girls are having a fight about a boyfriend; Selina needs quiet for budget negotiations
jokes.punchline: The absurdity of a VP shushing teenagers so she can negotiate hundreds of billions of federal dollars in the middle of a birthday party
jokes.explanation: Good situational comedy — the VP and the teenagers are parallel dramas, each undignified in different ways. Selina's oblivious equivalence between the two fights is the joke.

jokes.text: Mary: 'I cannot stand teenage girls.' / Selina: 'Were you ever one?' / Mary: 'Never.' / Selina: 'Me neither.'
jokes.setup: Both women are frustrated by teenage girl drama interrupting their negotiations
jokes.punchline: 'Were you ever one?' / 'Never.' / 'Me neither.' — both of them rejecting the premise of ever having been young or vulnerable
jokes.explanation: Three-line masterpiece of economy. Both women deny their entire adolescence simultaneously, revealing their mutual identity as career-defined political animals who have psychologically excised the vulnerable phases of life. The deadpan agreement ('Me neither') transforms what could be a throwaway into a genuine character revelation. Perfect for both characters.

jokes.text: Mike singing: 'Amy and Ed work for the VP, K-I-S-S-I-N-G.' / Amy: 'Fuck off, Mike.' / Mike: 'Sure.'
jokes.setup: Mike notices Amy and Ed together at the party
jokes.punchline: Mike sings a kindergarten rhyme in a highly professional political setting; Amy's dismissal; Mike's cheerful 'Sure.'
jokes.explanation: Mike's playground-level teasing in a high-stakes political environment is consistent character comedy. Mike's acquiescent 'Sure' to 'Fuck off' is the better beat — he's happy to have made the joke and doesn't need a win.

jokes.text: Jonah: 'I wonder how much vodka we'd need to have to have a little Mrs. Robinson moment.' / 'That is extremely disrespectful.' / Jonah: 'That's like the highest compliment you can give somebody.'
jokes.setup: Jonah and presumably Mike are watching Selina at the party and Jonah makes a Mrs. Robinson reference
jokes.punchline: Jonah genuinely believes proposing a seduction fantasy is the highest compliment — and can't understand why it's disrespectful
jokes.explanation: Jonah's confident wrongness is a reliable formula. The structure (disrespectful claim + genuine bewilderment that it's disrespectful + wrong justification) is clean Jonah comedy.

jokes.text: Jonah revealing to Ed that Amy and Dan used to date: 'Amy and Dan used to date, FYI.' / Dan: 'Yeah, thanks for the backgrounding, Jonah.'
jokes.setup: Jonah casually drops the Amy/Dan relationship history to Ed, Amy's current date
jokes.punchline: Dan's dry 'Thanks for the backgrounding, Jonah' — using political jargon ('backgrounding' = opposition research briefing) to express contempt
jokes.explanation: Dan's weaponization of political jargon as insult is crisp. 'Backgrounding' is exactly the right word — it implies Jonah is an opposition researcher working against Dan, which is accurate.

jokes.text: Jonah's logic that calling the VP a 'Mrs. Robinson' is 'the highest compliment you can give somebody'
jokes.setup: Someone calls Jonah out for being disrespectful about the VP
jokes.punchline: Jonah genuinely believes Mrs. Robinson is an unqualified compliment
jokes.explanation: Jonah's inability to parse the inherent condescension in his 'compliment' is consistent character comedy.

jokes.text: Jonah to Dan: 'Does that mean you're back on the market?' / Dan: 'I'm sorry, are you hitting on my date during my date?' / Jonah: 'Are you on meds?' / 'Yeah, antibiotics. Just keeping fresh. I'm not mentally ill if that's what you're implying.'
jokes.setup: Jonah has just torpedoed the date by revealing Dan's history; Dan confronts him
jokes.punchline: 'Are you on meds?' / 'Yeah, antibiotics. Just keeping fresh.' — and the completely unnecessary defensive 'I'm not mentally ill' that implies maybe he is
jokes.explanation: 'Just keeping fresh' about antibiotics is a strange, funny phrase. Then his unprompted 'I'm not mentally ill if that's what you're implying' — when no one implied that — is a perfect Jonah move: the protesteth-too-much that raises exactly the question he's trying to dismiss.

jokes.text: Selina's birthday speech that gradually collapses into proposing 'constructive talking time' and asking for 'chillaxing kind of music'
jokes.setup: Selina begins a birthday speech for Catherine at the microphone
jokes.punchline: The speech disintegrates from personal warmth into vague management-speak about 'constructive talking time' and instructions about when to play music, never actually getting to a heartfelt sentiment
jokes.explanation: Extended cringe sequence where Selina tries to give a birthday toast but her political-speak takes over — she offers 'constructive talking time' as a gift to her daughter. The timestamp beats (each [MM:SS] marker showing another rambling beat of the non-speech) are crucial here. 'Chillaxing kind of music' is the absurd verbal collapse of a woman who cannot access genuine emotion under pressure. Beautifully Selina.

jokes.text: Amy on the Meyer the Liar story: 'Monsters. It's like first Princess Diana, now this.'
jokes.setup: Amy is frustrated by the press coverage about Selina and Andrew
jokes.punchline: Amy comparing the tabloid scrutiny of Selina's relationship to Princess Diana — completely disproportionate self-victimization
jokes.explanation: The Princess Diana comparison is characteristically Veep in its grandiosity — Selina and her team always frame themselves as historical victims. The gap between Diana's actual tragedy and a politician's PR headache is where the comedy lives.

jokes.text: Ben: 'As people say, if it rhymes, it chimes.' / Mike: 'Who says that? No one says that.' / Ben: 'I'm pretty sure I heard someone say that.'
jokes.setup: Ben is trying to explain the staying power of 'Meyer the Liar' as a narrative
jokes.punchline: 'If it rhymes, it chimes' is an invented platitude that Ben delivers as folk wisdom; Mike immediately and correctly identifies that no one says it; Ben refuses to retreat
jokes.explanation: The exchange is small but precise. Ben invents an aphorism mid-sentence and sells it as established wisdom; Mike's instant and correct debunking ('No one says that') is textbook straight-man work; Ben's stubborn 'I'm pretty sure I heard someone say that' is perfect — he won't abandon his invented folk saying.

jokes.text: Selina: 'I'm swimming in it and I'm breathing it in through my snorkel.'
jokes.setup: Ben says the Meyer the Liar narrative is 'an avalanche of shit' and Selina agrees
jokes.punchline: Selina's extension of the metaphor — she's not just in the avalanche of shit, she's actively snorkeling through it — the absurd specificity of the snorkel
jokes.explanation: Good metaphor extension — the snorkel is the specific detail that makes it funny. The image of Selina actively breathing shit through a snorkel suggests she's not just a victim but is somehow equipped for this, which is darker and funnier.

jokes.text: Ben: 'Oh, great. Here's Eeyore. Deal, boom. Party, boom. You are the boom boom veep.'
jokes.setup: Ben arrives in a bad moment where the team is stressed about the Meyer the Liar narrative
jokes.punchline: Ben's breezy celebration of Selina's wins ('boom boom veep') as he apparently walked into a very different conversation
jokes.explanation: The 'boom boom veep' is a fun coinage, and 'Here's Eeyore' is a good entrance line. Slightly undermined by being a transitional scene beat rather than a standalone joke.

jokes.text: Andrew at the dinner, about free diving: 'Kind of like sitting next to my mother after Thanksgiving dinner.'
jokes.setup: Andrew is describing free diving (holding your breath as long as possible)
jokes.punchline: Comparing holding your breath to the ordeal of sitting next to his flatulent/unpleasant mother after a large meal
jokes.explanation: Clean setup/punchline with good economy. The implied reason for holding your breath (mother's post-Thanksgiving flatulence) is left to the imagination, which is the right call. Works as character flavoring for Andrew — casually inappropriate about family.

jokes.text: Catherine: 'Mom, you're doing your fake laugh again.' / Selina: 'What?' / 'Come on, this is nice.' / 'I can't tell if it's fake.' / 'You could never tell if it was fake.' / Andrew: 'Yes, I could.' / 'Mm-hmm. What are you, some sort of expert?' / 'In certain areas.'
jokes.setup: Catherine calls out Selina's performative laughter during the flirtation with Andrew
jokes.punchline: Andrew's claim to be an expert in 'certain areas' — ostensibly about knowing Selina's fake laugh, but loaded with double meaning
jokes.explanation: Well-constructed exchange. Catherine's 'I can't tell if it's fake' suggests Selina's artificiality runs so deep even her daughter can't read her. Andrew's 'In certain areas' is good — it works on multiple levels simultaneously and is suitably uncomfortable at a family dinner.

jokes.text: Dan to Amy about Andrew/Selina flirting: 'This is not flirting. This is like as subtle as putting a nude photo on Facebook.'
jokes.setup: Andrew says 'This is flirting' about his and Selina's dynamic; Dan disagrees
jokes.punchline: The Facebook nude photo comparison — not just unsubtle but specifically digital-age unsubtle, the kind of thing you can never take back
jokes.explanation: Dan's observation is precise and funny — the Facebook nude photo is exactly the right contemporary analog for weaponized exhibitionism. It also reveals Dan's jealousy through his overinvested attention to Selina/Andrew dynamics.

jokes.text: Amy to Dan: 'I heard whining and I assumed I needed to apologize. How long have you not been listening to me?'
jokes.setup: Dan says 'I'm sorry' for something; Amy probes why
jokes.punchline: 'I heard whining and I assumed I needed to apologize' — a Pavlovian reflex to Dan's general vibe; the follow-up question ('How long have you not been listening?') adds a second layer
jokes.explanation: Sharp Amy line — she's been so conditioned to Dan's neediness that she reflexively apologizes for his general emotional state. Her 'how long have you not been listening' clarifies she knows exactly what kind of relationship this is.

jokes.text: Andrew: 'I can't stand that alpha male arrogant thing.' / 'I really can't stand it, Andrew.' / '...And fuck you for thinking so.'
jokes.setup: Selina calls Andrew out for his alpha-male arrogance in anticipating her order
jokes.punchline: Andrew agrees, apologizes, then immediately says 'And fuck you for thinking so' — completely undermining the apology
jokes.explanation: Excellent Andrew character writing. The triple beat — agrees with criticism, apologizes ('Forgive me'), THEN says 'And fuck you for thinking so' — is a perfect encapsulation of his charm/aggression cycle. The apology is real AND the anger is real, simultaneously. Highly quotable and character-defining.

jokes.text: Andrew: 'And I'm very much enjoying the plastered-on smile, pretending everything's okay because people are watching.'
jokes.setup: Andrew and Selina are having a fight at the dinner table in public
jokes.punchline: Andrew narrating the performance they're both putting on — exposing the meta-layer of their public performance of togetherness
jokes.explanation: Andrew's self-awareness makes him a different class of opponent for Selina — he sees the performance and names it. The comedy is the double-level: they're fighting about the performance while performing.

jokes.text: Jonah to Ed: 'So, Ed. Eddie. Who do you know? Do you know Ray McCaskill? We both are really into '80s buddy comedies, which is ironically how we became buddies.' / 'Fuck buddies.' / Jonah: 'Why are you here? You really don't have anything to do, do you?'
jokes.setup: Jonah is doing his oblivious networking at the dinner table, bonding with Ed over '80s buddy comedies
jokes.punchline: Jonah's cheerful: 'which is ironically how we became buddies' — using 'ironically' incorrectly (it's not ironic at all); then Dan's 'Fuck buddies' is the sniper shot
jokes.explanation: Jonah's misuse of 'ironically' is consistent character comedy. Dan's 'Fuck buddies' is a perfect two-word grenade. The follow-up question — 'You really don't have anything to do, do you?' — is a good button.

jokes.text: Amy and Andrew have a two-sentence exchange about love-hate: 'I understand that you guys have a love-hate relationship, but most people alternate emotions. They don't experience them at the same time.' / 'We've always had a volatile relationship.' / 'By that you mean bat shit crazy.'
jokes.setup: Amy is trying to make sense of Selina and Andrew's dynamic
jokes.punchline: 'By that you mean bat shit crazy' — Amy's translator function, converting Andrew's euphemism into plain English
jokes.explanation: Amy's role as clear-eyed explainer of Veep chaos is well-served here. 'Bat shit crazy' as the translation for 'volatile' is accurate and the speed of her correction is good.

jokes.text: Andrew: 'Followed by long, hard... making up, right?' / Catherine: 'Okay, I feel like you're actually going to have sex on this table and that would truly ruin my birthday.' / Selina: 'Catherine, please. Nobody's having sex on the table.'
jokes.setup: Andrew is describing their fights, doing a long pause before 'making up' to imply sex
jokes.punchline: Catherine's flat 'that would truly ruin my birthday' — treating the possibility of parental table sex as a genuine practical concern rather than a joke
jokes.explanation: Catherine's deadpan pragmatism ('that would truly ruin my birthday') is the best moment for her character — treating the prospect of parental sex on a restaurant table as a logistical birthday problem, not an emotional one. Then Selina's 'Nobody's having sex on the table' is the perfect denial that somehow validates the concern. Great three-person escalation beat.

jokes.text: Selina: 'I mean, we are sexual beings, of course.' / 'Oh, my God.' / 'We had sex. We, you know-- I mean, great sex.' / 'Great sex.' / Catherine: 'Well, I'm going to have to text my therapist.'
jokes.setup: Selina, after denying sex on the table, then doubles down on discussing their sex life
jokes.punchline: Catherine's 'I'm going to have to text my therapist' — the only rational response to one's parents enthusiastically discussing their great sex at one's 21st birthday dinner
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation — Selina 'solves' the sex-on-the-table crisis by volunteering detailed information about their sex life, which is worse. 'Great sex' chorused by both parents is peak cringe. Catherine's therapist text is the exact right punctuation. This and the previous beat function as a two-part escalation.

jokes.text: Andrew: 'Great sex.' (echoing Selina's 'great sex') / Catherine: 'Well, I'm going to have to text my therapist.'
jokes.setup: Selina and Andrew are competing to affirm how great their sex life was
jokes.punchline: Catherine's flat, resigned 'I'm going to have to text my therapist' — acknowledging the irreparable psychological damage in real time
jokes.explanation: Catherine's mid-conversation therapist-text is a perfect modern punchline — the act of needing to text your therapist in real time because your parents are discussing their 'great sex' at your birthday dinner is both darkly funny and completely believable. Exceptional economy and character integration for Catherine, who deserves more credit as a comedic character in this episode.

jokes.text: Jonah to someone who claims to be a Quaker: 'Bullshit. No one's a fucking Quaker. You probably think that staying sober keeps you on top of your game. Well, guess what. I work hard and I play hard, bitch. That's my credo. I got that shit tattooed on my dick with room to spare.'
jokes.setup: Someone at the party doesn't drink; Jonah is offended
jokes.punchline: 'I got that shit tattooed on my dick with room to spare' — the escalation from life credo to penile self-advertisement to a size boast, all delivered with absolute conviction
jokes.explanation: Pure Jonah. The escalation from 'no one's a Quaker' to 'tattooed on my dick with room to spare' covers a lot of ground quickly. The 'room to spare' size claim delivered without irony is exactly Jonah. The structure — claim to a credo, say it's tattooed, add the size boast — is efficient and well-paced.

jokes.text: Jonah: 'I work hard and I play hard, bitch. That's my credo. I got that shit tattooed on my dick with room to spare.'
jokes.setup: Jonah is defending his lifestyle philosophy to Ed
jokes.punchline: 'I got that shit tattooed on my dick with room to spare' — the 'room to spare' implication is devastatingly ambiguous
jokes.explanation: The 'room to spare' clause is doing enormous comedic work — it implies either (a) his dick is very large or (b) the motto is very short. Context and Jonah's character suggest both simultaneously, making it maximally funny. The structure is perfect: the 'bitch' coda on 'that's my credo' already lands, and then 'tattooed on my dick' escalates, and 'with room to spare' is the perfect button. Highly quotable, exceptional Jonah character integration.

jokes.text: Dan's Jonah takedown: 'Jonah, you're not even a man. You're like an early draft of a man where they just sketched out a giant mangled skeleton, but they didn't have time to add details like pigment or self-respect. You're Frankenstein's monster if his monster was made entirely of dead dicks.'
jokes.setup: Dan responding to Jonah's boasting and general presence
jokes.punchline: 'Frankenstein's monster if his monster was made entirely of dead dicks' — the concluding image of a monster constructed from dead dicks is the punchline of a three-stage escalating insult
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best joke, and one of the finest insults in Veep's history. The three-stage structure is immaculate: (1) you're not a man, (2) you're an early draft of a man — which sets up a framework, (3) 'Frankenstein's monster if his monster was made entirely of dead dicks' — which detonates it. Each stage escalates the dehumanization. The 'pigment or self-respect' line is a detail that earns the finale. 'Made entirely of dead dicks' is the punchline that no reasonable person could see coming. Quotable out of context, devastating in context. Genuinely exceptional comedy writing — comfortably in the top tier of sitcom insult comedy.

jokes.text: Jonah: 'I'm gonna go to the bathroom. Just try not to embarrass yourselves while I'm gone.' / 'We'll give it a shot.'
jokes.setup: Jonah retreats after the devastating insult, trying to save face
jokes.punchline: 'We'll give it a shot' — a completely flat acknowledgment that plays as total contempt
jokes.explanation: Good button on the Jonah insult sequence — the dismissiveness of 'We'll give it a shot' conveys that no one in the room takes Jonah seriously enough to even engage with his parting shot.

jokes.text: Amy to Andrew when he tries to stop the Amy/Selina conversation about the bad news: 'I'm sorry. Can I interrupt a second?' / 'Uh, no.'
jokes.setup: Andrew politely tries to interrupt a work conversation
jokes.punchline: Amy's flat 'Uh, no' — no elaboration, no softening, just denial
jokes.explanation: Economy at its best — 'Uh, no' is two words doing maximum work. Amy's total dismissal of the ex-husband who outranks her socially is good character assertion.

jokes.text: Selina learning Andrew's PAC funded both sides: 'I just was fluffed and then I got fucked.' — and then: 'Do you know-- smile. Smile, you stupid asshole. Make it bigger.'
jokes.setup: Selina has just learned Andrew's company funded her opponent too
jokes.punchline: Selina invoking her own earlier Andrew characterization ('fluffed then fucked') proves Gary right, then immediately having to perform happiness for the cameras — 'Smile, you stupid asshole. Make it bigger.'
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the episode's central 'fluff/fuck' motif. Selina unknowingly quoted Gary's earlier warning — and it lands with full dramatic and comedic force. The immediate pivot to 'Smile, you stupid asshole. Make it bigger.' while fuming is a perfect Veep image: political performance weaponized against genuine human emotion. The instruction to herself (or Andrew) to smile bigger is both cruel and funny.

jokes.text: Selina to Andrew, while smiling for cameras: 'Do you know-- smile. Smile, you stupid asshole. Make it bigger. There you go. Do you have any idea how the press is all over me right now? You're a stupid fucking prick.'
jokes.setup: Selina has just learned Andrew's company funded her opponent; she's furious but being photographed
jokes.punchline: The contrast between the smile she's demanding and the volcanic fury of her language — 'Make it bigger. There you go.' punctuating 'you stupid asshole'
jokes.explanation: The instruction to smile while calling someone a 'stupid fucking prick' is a perfect Veep set piece — politics forcing performance over authentic emotion. The smile-coaching interrupting the insults ('Make it bigger. There you go.') is brilliant structural comedy: the political training is so deeply ingrained it runs simultaneously with genuine rage.

jokes.text: Andrew: 'You're a stupid fucking prick.' / 'Right? You say it.' / 'I'm not gonna say that.'
jokes.setup: Selina calls Andrew a 'stupid fucking prick' through her widening fake smile; then asks him to repeat the insult
jokes.punchline: Andrew refuses to say it, forcing Selina to retain ownership of the insult — and they are both smiling through all of this
jokes.explanation: The smiling fight is a visual joke that needs the performance. The transcript captures it in the surrounding stage directions. The idea of asking the person you're insulting to echo the insult back is a weird power-play that works.

jokes.text: Jonah explaining the bad news: 'I think it looks like you guys are going to stab each other with steak knives.' / Selina: 'We're having a fight and we are smiling and putting a face on and you can do that, too. There you go.'
jokes.setup: A party photographer/observer has caught what looks like a fight
jokes.punchline: Selina calmly explaining the performance they're doing and telling Jonah to join in — inducting him into the facade mid-crisis
jokes.explanation: Good comedic image — Selina coaching people through a performance of happiness while genuinely furious. Jonah's observation ('going to stab each other with steak knives') is accurate and good.

jokes.text: Amy tells Selina that POTUS is going to stall on the deal. Selina says: 'Oh, to congratulate me 'cause I made the deal last night.' / 'America thanks you.' / 'You're welcome.'
jokes.setup: Amy says there's a call from Kent; Selina assumes it's congratulations
jokes.punchline: 'America thanks you.' / 'You're welcome.' — then the immediate reveal that POTUS is going to betray her
jokes.explanation: Classic setup/misdirection/reversal. Selina's gracious 'You're welcome' to America (spoken to Amy) is the peak of her delusion before reality crashes in. Good structural beat before the bad news.

jokes.text: Selina on POTUS betrayal: 'Fuck POTUS. Right? And fuck Kent. They're just a bunch of fuckers.' — and then realizing she may have been photographed saying this.
jokes.setup: Selina vents about POTUS betraying her on the deal
jokes.punchline: Immediately noticing she may have been caught: 'Did they just get that? Me being angry?' — and the attempt to clarify who the anger was directed at
jokes.explanation: The classic Veep trap: say something devastatingly honest, get caught. Selina's clarification that she was angry about POTUS not Andrew is the political damage control instinct kicking in mid-panic. Good character rhythm.

jokes.text: Selina: 'I was angry with you before, but I'm not angry with you now.' / Andrew: 'I understand that, honey.' / 'Don't call me honey for starters.'
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to explain her anger is directed at POTUS, not Andrew (for once)
jokes.punchline: Andrew immediately calls her 'honey' in sympathy — which immediately gets him yelled at again
jokes.explanation: A good beat — Andrew can't even attempt solidarity without triggering Selina. 'Don't call me honey for starters' implies a list of offenses, and 'for starters' is the right qualifier.

jokes.text: Andrew tries to help Selina control the situation by saying 'I think that if you guys continue to smile, you're actually gonna get permanent lockjaw.' / Selina: 'She's so funny.' / Catherine: 'Sarcastic.'
jokes.setup: Everyone is frozen in fake smiles trying to manage the PR disaster
jokes.punchline: Andrew's 'lockjaw' observation; Selina praising him; Catherine flatly correcting 'Sarcastic'
jokes.explanation: Small but effective — Catherine's 'Sarcastic' one-word correction of Selina's warmth toward Andrew keeps the reality check coming. Lockjaw image is good.

jokes.text: The birthday cake blowout — Selina: 'Make a wish.' / Andrew blows out candles. / 'You can make your wish in the car.' — as everyone flees the venue
jokes.setup: Catherine's birthday party has descended into a full PR crisis evacuation; the cake arrives at the worst moment
jokes.punchline: 'You can make your wish in the car' — the birthday wish optimistically relocated to the escape vehicle
jokes.explanation: 'You can make your wish in the car' is a perfectly compressed image of Selina's parenting — birthday magic deferred to the evacuation vehicle. The gap between what a 21st birthday is supposed to be and what this has become is the episode's emotional and comedic throughline.

jokes.text: Andrew jumps out of the car mid-argument: 'What? You're getting out here?' / 'Yep.' / 'What are you talking about?' / 'I've got to go.' / 'Wait. Andrew, wait. Wait. We need to talk.' / Andrew: 'Bye, honey. Happy birthday.' / 'God damn it.'
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to continue the fight in the car; Andrew simply exits
jokes.punchline: Andrew's breezy 'Bye, honey. Happy birthday' to Catherine as he vanishes mid-crisis — the ultimate Andrew move: charming, irresponsible, blameless
jokes.explanation: The physical comedy of Andrew simply exiting a moving argument works because it's so Andrew — he literally extracts himself from situations he's created. 'Bye, honey. Happy birthday' to Catherine is perfect: warm, charming, and landing like a punch because it's said in the middle of chaos of his making.

jokes.text: Final scene: Gary finds Catherine alone with her birthday cake. Extended awkward silence as he tries to figure out what to say. 'Pretty nice cake you got there.' / '...' / 'You gonna eat all of it?' / Catherine offers him some. Gary: 'Jesus fucking Christ. Here. Take that. Carrot cake's good.' / 'You want to try it?' / 'Nope. It's all yours. I can use my phone to cut it.'
jokes.setup: The episode ends with Catherine alone at her ruined birthday party; Gary finds her
jokes.punchline: The entire exchange is the joke — Gary's attempt to connect with Catherine is about as deep as 'Pretty nice cake you got there,' and his offer to use his phone as a cake knife is both practical and desperately deflating
jokes.explanation: A beautifully dark button for the episode. The silence and halting small talk between Gary and Catherine over a birthday cake is Veep in miniature: political people who cannot do human comfort, trying anyway. 'I can use my phone to cut it' is the final image — technology substituting for actual care. The awkward silence IS the joke; every pause compounds the devastation of what this birthday party became.

jokes.text: Final scene: Gary sits alone with Catherine and the uncut birthday cake. Small talk. 'You gonna eat all of it?' / 'Do you want a piece?' / 'I do.' / 'Here. Carrot cake's good.' / 'You want to try it?' / 'Nope.' / 'I can use my phone to cut it.'
jokes.setup: The chaos has dispersed; Gary and Catherine are alone with the birthday cake in the wreckage of the party
jokes.punchline: The entire scene is the joke — the awkward domesticity of Gary offering to cut cake with his phone in the aftermath of a political and family catastrophe
jokes.explanation: The episode's tonal masterstroke. After all the political chaos, personal betrayals, and birthday devastation, Gary and Catherine sit alone with the cake. The bathos is total: Gary's 'Carrot cake's good' is one of the saddest, funniest lines in the episode. The offer to use his phone as a cake knife is absurdist and practical simultaneously — Gary always finds a workaround for Selina (or, in this case, Selina's abandoned daughter). The silence between lines is as funny as the lines. High character integration: Gary is now the most present parent figure at this party.

tmdb_overview: Catherine's 21st birthday party becomes a forum for Selina's budget negotiation with the majority leader, but Amy and Gary are more worried how Selina will handle seeing her ex-husband. Meanwhile, Mike discusses Selina's role in the Uzbek hostage crisis with the press.
insight: Sexual dysfunction plot escalates faster than Selina's composure crumbles.
